NFC Tag Writing Tool for Tabling and More
May 5, 2026
Lee
Wylie
she / they
Emerging Media & Technology
& Visual Communication Design
Grad Student
BFA, Carnegie Mellon University

Have 3D printed stuff?
That's probably made by me.

First, a quick term

What is NFC?

NFC
Near Field Communication

Tiny chips that share a small bit of information when something taps them. No batteries, no internet, no app. They only work from about four inches away.

Tap to pay at the register. Amiibo and Skylanders. The chip in your pet's collar. You've already used this.

The Problem

Two pain points, one project

01

People don't want more apps

When I hand someone an NFC keychain, the magic disappears the moment I say "download this app to set it up." Nobody wants a one-off app for a one-off task.

02

Writing tags one by one is painful

Existing tools are fussy and unreliable when you're writing a lot of tags at once. For selling keychains in bulk, I need something that just works without babysitting.

Why not QR codes?

NFC disappears into the object

A QR code has to live on the surface of whatever it's attached to. It takes up space, ages badly, and visually competes with the object it's on.

An NFC chip hides inside. The keychain stays clean. Tap it, and it just works.

  • QR-printed keychains use more filament and take longer to print.
  • If the link changes, a printed QR is dead. NFC can be rewritten.
  • QR needs a camera and aim. NFC just needs a tap.

Bring the writing process to the person.
No app to download. No instructions to remember.

The device walks you through every step on screen, right there at the table.

What it does

Six things the device has to do well

Type a URL

Use a regular keyboard. No touchscreen needed.

Write to the tag

Tap a keychain on the reader. The link is saved to it.

Test it on the spot

After writing, the device asks the user to tap the tag with their phone to make sure it works.

Clear feedback

The screen shows what's happening at every step, including when something goes wrong.

Bulk mode

Write the same link to a stack of tags in a row, no re-typing.

Privacy first

No personal info collected or saved. Links are forgotten as soon as they're written.

How it got here

Previous iterations

Where it started

Same Raspberry Pi, but a tiny character display and a tangle of exposed wiring. It worked — the display was the bottleneck.

What changed along the way

Earlier UI passes leaned on a fixed grid of platforms with no room for custom URLs and only the lightest guidance. Each round added more on-screen direction and softened the visual language.

Previous iterations, continued

Earlier screen designs

Earlier screens

Two passes from along the way: a fixed platform grid, and a welcome screen that did the heavy lifting before any on-screen guidance existed.

Under the hood

Python and web tools drive the interface, coded with Claude Code and ChatGPT Codex.

The current iteration is up next — live.

Live Demo

The Kiosk Interface

User Testing

User testing, so far and ahead

So far: informal

People have used the device casually, which has surfaced rough edges and pointed at where the on-screen guidance needs to be clearer. Useful, but not structured.

Ahead: formal

Sit-down sessions with think-aloud feedback, focused on two questions: can someone use this without help, and could someone else run it at their own event?

Going forward

  • A proper enclosure for the hardware.
  • Letting people copy and paste links from their phone.
  • Going beyond links — writing vCards (digital business cards).
Wrapping up

Part of a larger build.

The NFC writer feeds directly into a 3D printing business I'm building, as a product, as an internal tool for tabling, and as a way to add value to the keychains themselves.

Take an NFC keychain on the way out. Tap it on your phone and it'll take you to the project page, where the slides and a deeper write-up live.
